title: Predictable Mean-Field Chaos in Random Recurrent Networks
authors: Alkesh Yadav, Vladimir Shaidurov, Jonathan Kadmon
abstract: Dynamical mean-field theory recasts deterministic chaos in random recurrent networks as an effective stochastic process. We show that for analytic nonlinearities with sufficiently fast Fourier decay, this stochasticity is only apparent: the continuous past of a realized mean-field trajectory uniquely determines its future. The mean-field theory is therefore not merely an ensemble description, but a conditional prediction theory for individual trajectories. Unfolding the power spectrum into a Krylov state space exposes how this latent determinism is organized across an infinite hierarchy of temporal modes. The associated Krylov growth rate sets the complexity of finite-resolution prediction and upper-bounds the largest Lyapunov exponent in this class of networks. Thus, microscopic sensitivity and predictive complexity are distinct aspects of mean-field chaos. Our results extend Krylov growth ideas developed for Hamiltonian chaotic dynamics to classical dissipative systems.
summary: This paper investigates the nature of 'chaos' in random recurrent networks by treating the system's dynamics as a stochastic process derived from DMFT. It demonstrates that if the underlying nonlinearity is sufficiently smooth (e.g., Gaussian), the apparent chaos is actually perfectly predictable due to latent determinism revealed through spectral analysis. The work establishes quantitative relationships between prediction complexity ($\alpha$) and sensitivity ($\lambda_L$).
main_problem: To determine if 'mean-field chaos' in random recurrent networks is truly chaotic or possesses latent determinism based on its continuous past.
main_result: For analytic nonlinearities with fast Fourier decay, the system exhibits perfect predictability ($\sigma^2_\infty(	au_p) = 0$), meaning the future is uniquely determined by the past. The Krylov growth rate ($\alpha$) and Lyapunov exponent ($\lambda_L$) are distinct measures of complexity.
method: The analysis uses Dynamical Mean-Field Theory (DMFT) combined with concepts from Gaussian processes, spectral analysis, and unfolding the autocorrelation function into a Krylov state space.
model_or_system: The study analyzes large random recurrent neural networks, specifically using the Sompolinsky–Crisanti–Sommers (SCS) model dynamics. The core mathematical tools involve analytic continuation of the autocorrelation function $\Delta(	au)$ and mapping it to an effective potential $V(\Delta)$.
key_observables: Prediction error variance ($\sigma^2_\infty(	au_p)$), Krylov growth rate ($\alpha$), Largest Lyapunov exponent ($\lambda_L$), and the singularity location ($t_S$) in the complex time plane.
important_parameters: The decay rate of the activation function's Fourier transform (e.g., Gaussian vs. $	anh$); disorder strength ($g-g_c$).
main_assumptions: Perfect predictability relies critically on the assumption that the activation function $\phi(x)$ has a Gaussian or faster Fourier decay; finite prediction requires approximating unresolved tails as Ornstein-Uhlenbeck processes.
figures_summary: Figures compare Krylov growth rate ($\alpha$) and largest Lyapunov exponent ($\lambda_L$) across different nonlinearities, illustrating how $\alpha \ge \lambda_L$. Other figures show the analytic continuation of potentials and prediction error decay over time/frequency.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the link between chaos and determinism via spectral analysis (Paley-Wiener class). It then develops the mathematical machinery using Krylov state spaces to quantify predictability ($\alpha$) versus sensitivity ($\lambda_L$). Finally, it analyzes how the nonlinearity's analytic structure dictates whether this prediction is perfect or merely apparent.
why_interesting: The work provides a rigorous framework for distinguishing between true stochasticity and deterministic chaos in complex dynamical systems, drawing parallels between classical chaotic dynamics (Hamiltonian) and dissipative neural network models. This methodology of using spectral analysis to probe latent determinism is highly relevant across physics disciplines.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Analytic structure of the smooth chaotic mean-field process. (a) Analytic continuation of the effec- tive potential V (∆) for ϕ(x) = erf(x). Color denotes phase and contours denote magnitude; the nearest branch point lies at ∆= S > ∆0, outside the physical interval of autocorrela- tions. Inset: V (∆) on the real axis. (b) Analytic continuation of the autocorrelation ∆(τ) in complex time. The dashed lines mark the analytic strip | Im τ| < tS. Inset: the PSD ˜∆(ω) on a semilog scale, showing the exponential tail set by the sin- gularity at tS.

FIG. 2. Krylov growth and finite-resolution predic- tion. (a) Krylov growth rate α (solid) and largest Lya- punov exponent λL (dashed) versus distance from the chaotic transition, g −gc, for smooth odd nonlinearities. Colors de- note erf(x) and smoothed tanh(x). (b) Same comparison for smoothed ReLU, showing α > λL throughout the chaotic regime. (c) Chaotic mean-field spectrum ˜∆(ω) for the sech kernel and p-mode Markov approximations. (d) Conditional forecast from finitely resolved Krylov data; line, conditional mean; shading, ±2 standard deviations. (e) Conditional prediction error Ep(t) for increasing resolution p.
